index.html
A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Data Analysis Preface Understanding the Terminology: Vibe Coding vs. Vibe Analysis Why Does This Approach Matter? Core Principles of This Approach

A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Data Analysis Rishabh Kumar | The views and opinions expressed in this book are those of the author alone and do not represent the views of his employer. 2026-04-28 Preface Welcome to A Practical Guide to AI-Assisted Data Analysis. This book is about a simple but consequential idea: the way we write code for data analysis is changing fundamentally, and the skills required to do it well are changing with it. “Vibe coding”; the practice of using natural language to instruct AI models to generate executable code; has captured the imagination of the software world. Tools like GitHub Copilot can translate a plain English comment into a working function in seconds. For building applications, this is a revolution. For extracting analytical insights, it is a loaded gun. The difference matters. A vibe-coded web application with a minor UI bug still works. A vibe-coded data pipeline with a silent aggregation error produces numbers that look perfectly plausible but are fundamentally wrong. Decisions get made, reports get published, and nobody realises the foundation was flawed until it is too late. Vibe Analysis is the methodology this book proposes to bridge that gap. It takes the speed and accessibility of vibe coding and layers on the rigour that analytical work demands: structured code reviews, statistical validation, explicit assumption-testing, and human ownership of every result. If you spend more time trying to remember the exact arguments of dplyr::pivot_longer() or the aesthetics of a ggplot2 chart than thinking about the insights your data contains, this book is for you. If you manage a team of analysts who have started using AI tools and you want to know what questions to ask, this book is also for you. Understanding the Terminology: Vibe Coding vs. Vibe Analysis To ensure clarity throughout this book, we distinguish two related but critically different concepts: Vibe Coding: The general mechanism of writing software using natural language prompts. It prioritises speed, compilation, and whether the code runs. Vibe Analysis: The rigorous methodology of applying that mechanism to data science. It introduces statistical rigour, code reviews drawn from the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), and hypothesis testing to ensure the rapid code generation does not produce hallucinated insights. The Contrast in Workflow The following chart illustrates why extracting insights requires a different, more rigorous workflow than building standard software applications: Why Does This Approach Matter? In traditional data analysis, the bottleneck is often the translation of a statistical idea into programming syntax. You have a question (“How do sales compare across regions over the last three quarters?”), but you must spend time writing boilerplate code to group, summarise, and plot the data. With AI assistants; specifically GitHub Copilot, the primary tool explored in this book; you can write the question in plain English as a comment, and the assistant will synthesise the code. Your role as an analyst shifts: From typist to architect: You define the analytical strategy and the questions worth asking. From debugger to auditor: You review and validate the logic the AI proposes, rather than writing it from scratch. From memoriser to thinker: You focus on research design and statistical validity rather than syntax recall. This shift is powerful, but only if you maintain the discipline to verify what the AI produces. The chapters that follow will show you how. Core Principles of This Approach To get the most out of GitHub Copilot in your data analysis workflow, adopt these core practices from the outset: Plan, Act, Review: Slice your analytical tasks into smaller, manageable steps. Do not ask for a full dashboard in a single prompt. Ask for data import, then cleaning, then visualisation; reviewing the output at each stage. Context is King: The AI needs to see your data structure. Print the head() or str() of your data frame so Copilot understands the variable names and types before generating transformations. Human-in-the-Loop: Always verify intermediate steps. A model might confidently generate code that looks correct but performs an aggregation incorrectly; for example, using mean() when you needed median(). Create Persistent Context: Document your data assumptions in a PROJECT.md file or at the top of your script. This gives Copilot a consistent reference point and keeps it aligned with your goals across sessions. In the subsequent chapters, we will dive into setting up your environment, writing effective prompts for data wrangling, and iterating on visualisations entirely through conversation.
getting-started.html
Chapter 1 Getting Started 1.1 Choosing Your IDE 1.2 Activating GitHub Copilot 1.3 Project-Level Copilot Instructions 1.4 Writing Your First Prompt

Chapter 1 Getting Started To master Vibe Analysis in R, your environment needs to be properly set up. You need a frictionless way to communicate with your AI pair programmer so that writing a prompt feels as fluid as writing native R code. 1.1 Choosing Your IDE While standard RStudio (Posit) is the classic choice for R users, the advent of AI assistants has made Visual Studio Code (VS Code) a highly competitive alternative. RStudio: Recent versions of RStudio include native integration with GitHub Copilot. Setup is as straightforward as checking an option in the Global Options menu. The interface is optimized specifically for R, which handles environments and plotting seamlessly. VS Code: Provides deeper customization and often earlier access to the most advanced Copilot features (like Copilot Chat). The vscode-R extension has made VS Code a robust R IDE. For the purpose of this book, we assume you are using GitHub Copilot as your assistant, integrated into whichever IDE you prefer. 1.2 Activating GitHub Copilot Once you have your IDE set up, you’ll need an active GitHub Copilot subscription. In RStudio: 1. Go to Tools -> Global Options -> Copilot. 2. Check “Enable GitHub Copilot”. 3. Sign in to your GitHub account when prompted. In VS Code: 1. Install the “GitHub Copilot” extension. 2. Install the “GitHub Copilot Chat” extension for interactive conversations. 3. Sign in to GitHub through the bottom-left account icon. 1.3 Project-Level Copilot Instructions To write code in a consistent style across your project, you can provide general guidance to GitHub Copilot by creating an instructions file in your workspace. For instance, in VS Code or GitHub Copilot, you can specify custom instructions in a file like .github/copilot-instructions.md. This ensures that the AI defaults to your preferred libraries and formatting styles (such as strongly preferring tidyverse over base R). Here is an example of what that markdown file might contain to enforce consistent R and Python code styles: # General Guidance - Prioritize readability and maintainability. - Add comments explaining *why* something is done, not just *what*. # R Specific Guidelines - ALWAYS use `tidyverse` (e.g., `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`, `readr`) instead of base R functions. For example, use `read_csv()` rather than `read.csv()`. - Use the pipe operator `%>%` (or `|>`) to chain functions sequentially. - Use `snake_case` for all variable and function names. # Python Specific Guidelines - Use `pandas` for data manipulation, aiming for vectorized operations rather than base loops. - Use `snake_case` for variables and functions, and `PascalCase` for classes. - Provide type hints for all function arguments and return types. By adding these project-level instructions, Copilot will stay consistent with your team’s best practices out of the box, significantly reducing manual refactoring later. 1.4 Writing Your First Prompt With Copilot active, let’s write our first vibe code. Open a new R script. Let’s say you want to generate a random dataset to practice with. Instead of trying to remember the exact syntax for creating a data frame with random normal distributions, you simply write a comment describing what you want: # Create a data frame 'sales_data' with 100 rows. # Columns: 'date' (random days in 2023), 'region' (North, South, East, West), and 'revenue' (random normal dist, mean 500) Press Enter and wait a second. Copilot will synthesize the code in gray text (ghost text). If it looks correct, press Tab to accept it! # Create a data frame 'sales_data' with 100 rows. # Columns: 'date' (random days in 2023), 'region' (North, South, East, West), and 'revenue' (random normal dist, mean 500) sales_data <- data.frame( date = sample(seq(as.Date('2023/01/01'), as.Date('2023/12/31'), by="day"), 100, replace = TRUE), region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), 100, replace = TRUE), revenue = rnorm(100, mean = 500, sd = 100) ) You’ve just completed your first Vibe Analysis task. You expressed the intent, and Copilot provided the syntax. In the next chapter, we’ll explore the structured workflow for making this process reliable across more complex data manipulation.
the-vibe-workflow.html

Chapter 2 The Vibe Workflow 2.1 Applying the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) 2.2 The “Plan, Act, Review” Cycle 2.3 Prioritizing Context 2.4 Prompting for Data Manipulation (dplyr) 2.5 Adding Iterative Chaining

Chapter 2 The Vibe Workflow 2.1 Applying the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) In traditional Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC), teams move through distinct phases: requirements gathering, design, implementation, testing, and deployment. To ensure Vibe Analysis is high-quality and rigorous, we must adopt an SDLC mindset for our data projects. Instead of treating data analysis as an ad-hoc script, treat it as a software product. The AI writes the code, but you enforce the lifecycle: writing clear requirements (prompts), enforcing code reviews (auditing AI outputs), and rigorous testing (data validation and sanity checks). This structured approach minimizes hallucinations and ensures that the heavily automated coding process yields reliable, production-ready analytical pipelines. When analyzing data, standard manual coding requires translating every logical step into its corresponding R function (e.g., “I need group_by() then summarize()”). Vibe coding allows you to stay at the conceptual level, but only if you provide effective prompts and maintain a strong workflow. 2.2 The “Plan, Act, Review” Cycle Vibe Analysis is not magic; you are managing a very fast, sometimes overconfident junior analyst. You should adopt a “Plan, Act, Review” cycle: Plan: Break down the analysis into steps. Don’t ask for a final predictive model from a raw dataset in one prompt. Act: Write a clear prompt for the first step. Review: Examine the generated code. Does it use the libraries you want? Run the code and use glimpse() or head() to verify the result before moving to the next step. 2.3 Prioritizing Context GitHub Copilot is a context engine. It reads the files you have open and the code preceding your cursor. If Copilot doesn’t know what columns your dataset has, its suggestions will be educated guesses (i.e., hallucinations). Best Practice: Show, don’t tell. Let Copilot “read” your data structure by doing this at the top of your script: library(dplyr) # Assume we loaded 'sales_data' earlier. # str(sales_data) # 'data.frame': 100 obs. of 3 variables: # $ date : Date, format: "2023-04-12" "2023-08-22" ... # $ region : chr "North" "South" "East" "West" ... # $ revenue: num 480.5 512.3 640.1 498.2 ... By pasting the str() or glimpse() output as a comment, Copilot now knows exactly what columns exist. 2.4 Prompting for Data Manipulation (dplyr) When you want to wrangle data, use intent-based prompts that specify the library if you have a preference. # Using dplyr, calculate the total revenue and average revenue per region for Q1 2023. Copilot will read the context (your str output) and generate: q1_summary <- sales_data %>% filter(date >= "2023-01-01" & date <= "2023-03-31") %>% group_by(region) %>% summarize( total_rev = sum(revenue), avg_rev = mean(revenue) ) 2.5 Adding Iterative Chaining If the result is almost what you want, but you forgot a detail, just add another comment to chain onto the pipeline: # Now arrange the previous result in descending order by total_rev q1_summary <- q1_summary %>% arrange(desc(total_rev)) By working in chunks, reviewing the intermediate states, and feeding that state back as context, you maintain total control over your data analysis while typing a fraction of the code.
visualizing-with-vibes.html
Chapter 3 Visualizing with Vibes 3.1 Prompting for Plots 3.2 Iterating on the Design 3.3 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) 3.4 The Review Step for Visualisations

Chapter 3 Visualizing with Vibes Data visualization is arguably where vibe coding shines the brightest. The ggplot2 package is incredibly powerful, but its granular grammar of graphics (themes, scales, geoms, facetting) requires remembering hundreds of specific arguments. Finding the right syntax to rotate x-axis labels by 45 degrees or move the legend to the bottom often leads analysts to Google. With GitHub Copilot, you simply ask for the aesthetic. 3.1 Prompting for Plots Let’s assume we want to visualize the summary data we created in the last chapter. If you just write # Plot the data, Copilot might give you a standard, ugly base R plot. You must be an architect and give specific design orders. library(ggplot2) # Create a bar chart showing total revenue by region using the q1_summary data frame. # Specifics: # - Reorder the bars so the highest revenue is first. # - Fill the bars with a steelblue color. # - Use a minimal theme and remove the grid lines. # - Add a title "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region" and remove the x-axis label. Copilot will likely generate a perfectly structured ggplot pipeline: ggplot(q1_summary, aes(x = reorder(region, -total_rev), y = total_rev)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.title.x = element_blank() ) + labs( title = "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region", y = "Total Revenue" ) 3.2 Iterating on the Design Often, the first plot is roughly correct, but you spot a visual flaw. Instead of digging into the theme() documentation, you keep vibe coding. Imagine the revenue numbers are in the tens of thousands and look messy (e.g., 45000). # Update the previous plot to format the y-axis labels as dollars with commas (using the scales package). # Also, add comma-formatted data labels on top of each bar. The AI updates the code: library(scales) ggplot(q1_summary, aes(x = reorder(region, -total_rev), y = total_rev)) + geom_col(fill = "steelblue") + geom_text(aes(label = dollar(total_rev)), vjust = -0.5) + scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar) + # The AI handles the scales package syntax theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), axis.title.x = element_blank() ) + labs( title = "Q1 2023 Revenue by Region", y = "Total Revenue" ) 3.3 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) You can use vibe coding to rapid-fire through different visual hypotheses during EDA. # Create a scatter plot of revenue vs date from sales_data. # Add a loess trend line in red. # Facet the plot by region. By conversationalising the visualisation step, you reduce the friction between a question (“Is revenue trending up differently in the South?”) and the visual answer. 3.4 The Review Step for Visualisations Visualisations generated by Copilot are usually aesthetically correct, but visually appealing does not mean analytically correct. Before including any chart in a report, always perform a basic sanity check: Does the axis range make sense? A y-axis that starts at zero versus a truncated axis tells very different stories. Are the colours or groupings reflecting the right variable? AI can silently swap grouping aesthetics. Does the trend line (if any) use the right method? A loess line and a linear model look similar but imply very different relationships. In the next chapter, we move from exploratory visualisation into the more statistically demanding territory of predictive modelling; where the stakes of an AI hallucination are highest.
modeling-and-machine-learning.html
Chapter 4 Modeling and Machine Learning 4.1 Prompting for Tidymodels 4.2 Reviewing the Architecture 4.3 Evaluating the Model 4.4 What Can Go Wrong in Modelling

Chapter 4 Modeling and Machine Learning While data cleaning and visualization benefit greatly from vibe coding, machine learning requires much more caution. When prompting for statistical models, the risk of “hallucinations” (the AI confidently generating statistically invalid methods or misinterpreting data) increases significantly. The AI does not inherently understand the assumptions of linear regression or the concept of data leakage. You must be highly explicit about your research design. 4.1 Prompting for Tidymodels The tidymodels framework in R provides a structured, modern way to do machine learning, but its syntax can be verbose (recipes, specs, workflows). Copilot handles this verbosity well. Start by defining the split and the recipe clearly: library(tidymodels) # Create a data split for sales_data: 80% training, 20% testing. # Set the seed to 123 for reproducibility. set.seed(123) sales_split <- initial_split(sales_data, prop = 0.8) sales_train <- training(sales_split) sales_test <- testing(sales_split) Now, construct the recipe. Explain the exact transformations you want: # Create a tidymodels recipe predicting 'revenue' using all other variables. # - Extract the month and day of the week from the 'date' column. # - Remove the original 'date' column. # - Dummy encode all nominal predictors. 4.2 Reviewing the Architecture This is where the Review step of the “Plan, Act, Review” workflow is critical. Read the generated recipe. Did it dummy encode correctly? Did it handle missing values as you intended? If you blindly accept ML code from Copilot, you will likely encounter errors when you fit() the model or, worse, generate an invalid predictive model that looks fine on the surface. 4.3 Evaluating the Model Once you have fitted a workflow, you can ask Copilot to generate the evaluation metrics, which saves you from remembering the exact yardstick functions: # Assuming 'rf_fit' is our fitted tidymodels workflow. # Generate predictions on the sales_test data. # Calculate RMSE and RSQ for the predictions. By constraining Copilot to write the plumbing (the tidymodels syntax) while you define the architecture (the features, the model type, the validation strategy), you balance speed with statistical rigour. 4.4 What Can Go Wrong in Modelling Machine learning is the chapter of Vibe Analysis where the gap between “code that runs” and “code that is correct” is widest. Data Leakage: The AI may inadvertently include future information in the training features (e.g., using a post-event column as a predictor). Always inspect the recipe and ask yourself whether every feature could realistically be available at prediction time. Wrong Metric for the Problem: Copilot will default to common metrics like RMSE for regression and accuracy for classification. If your business problem has an imbalanced target class, accuracy is misleading. Explicitly ask for precision, recall, or AUC. Missing Cross-Validation: A model fitted on a single 80/20 split may produce results that look strong but are not reliable. Always request cross-validation folds for any model you intend to deploy. When something goes wrong in modelling, the AI’s error messages can be cryptic. The next chapter covers how to troubleshoot the most common failure patterns efficiently.
troubleshooting.html
Chapter 5 Troubleshooting 5.1 Types of Errors 5.2 The Chat is Your Debugger 5.3 The “Rubber Duck” Technique

Chapter 5 Troubleshooting Vibe Analysis is not a perfect process. You will encounter errors. Variables will be misspelled, arguments will be deprecated, and the AI will sometimes suggest functions from packages you haven’t loaded. Knowing how to troubleshoot AI-generated code is a fundamental skill of the Vibe Analysis workflow. 5.1 Types of Errors Syntax Errors: Missing parentheses or commas. Copilot is usually very good at avoiding these, but they happen if you edit the code manually after generation. Logic Errors: The code runs perfectly, but it groups by the wrong column or joins the wrong tables. These are the most dangerous. You must validate intermediate states. Hallucinated Packages/Functions: Copilot might suggest a function like calculate_revenue() thinking it represents a built-in function, when in reality it doesn’t exist. 5.2 The Chat is Your Debugger When you use GitHub Copilot, you are not limited to inline ghost text. If you are using RStudio’s Copilot integration or VS Code with Copilot Chat, you have a conversational debugger. If an error is thrown in your console: Error in `mutate()`: ℹ In argument: `sales = as.numeric(revenue)`. Caused by error: ! object 'revenue' not found Do not spend 15 minutes staring at the code trying to find the typo. Simply copy the error message, highlight the chunk of code that caused it, and paste it into Copilot Chat: “I’m getting this error: [paste error]. Here is my data str(): [paste str]. Fix the code.” Copilot will immediately recognize that revenue is misspelled in your data frame, or that you forgot to load the dataset, or that it generated the wrong column name earlier. 5.3 The “Rubber Duck” Technique Sometimes, the logic is just complex. If you are writing a custom simulation or a massive nested loop, Copilot might get confused. When the code gets messy, delete it. Go back to plain English. Write a multi-line comment essentially talking to the AI (or a rubber duck) about what you are trying to achieve, step-by-step. Let it generate the code one tiny block at a time. Forcing the AI; and yourself; to slow down and explain the logic often solves the problem.
case-studies-what-can-go-wrong.html
Chapter 6 Case Studies: What Can Go Wrong? 6.1 The Illusion of Productivity 6.2 Case Study 1: The “Happy Path” Data Join 6.3 Case Study 2: Hallucinated Business Logic 6.4 The Velocity Gap

Chapter 6 Case Studies: What Can Go Wrong? Vibe coding can act as a force multiplier, but blindly trusting AI to generate complex analytical logic without the rigor of Vibe Analysis exposes you to several well-documented risks. In this chapter, we explore realistic case studies and research demonstrating what happens when the “Review” phase of the vibe-coding workflow is skipped. 6.1 The Illusion of Productivity A significant psychological trap of the vibe coding mechanism is the “Illusion of Productivity.” When you use GitHub Copilot, you feel extremely productive because lines of code appear on your screen instantaneously. However, empirical evidence paints a more complicated picture. In a 2024 study by GitClear (“Coding on Copilot: 2023 Data Suggests Downward Pressure on Code Quality”), researchers analyzed 153 million changed lines of code authored between 2020 and 2023. They found that as AI assistance increased, “code churn”; the percentage of code pushed to a repo that is reverted or updated within two weeks; grew rapidly. Furthermore, empirical studies on pull request times echo this contradiction. A 2024 report by Uplevel (“Can Developers Navigate the Rabbit Hole?”) analyzed over 800 developers and found that while developers subjectively felt faster, those using Copilot were actually 19% slower to merge pull requests and introduced more bugs compared to those not using the tool. Why? Because generating the script took seconds, but identifying the subtle logical flaws the AI introduced took hours. Reading and debugging unfamiliar, AI-generated statistics pipelines is often harder than writing them yourself. 6.2 Case Study 1: The “Happy Path” Data Join The Context: An analyst was tasked with combining two datasets: a primary customer_transactions table and a regional_metadata table. They vibe-coded the following prompt: # Join the transaction data with the metadata to get the region for each customer. What Copilot Generated: combined_data <- customer_transactions %>% inner_join(regional_metadata, by = "customer_id") The Failure: The AI assumed an inner_join was appropriate. However, the regional_metadata table was incomplete. Because an inner_join drops unmatched rows, the analyst silently lost 15% of their transaction data. The code ran without any R errors, looking perfectly successful on the “happy path.” The Fix: The prompt lacking context caused the error. The analyst should have specified a left_join and added a validation step to check for dropped rows. 6.3 Case Study 2: Hallucinated Business Logic The Context: A data scientist wanted to calculate the churn rate for a SaaS product. # Calculate the monthly churn rate for the platform. What Copilot Generated: churn_summary <- user_data %>% group_by(month) %>% summarize( total_users = n(), churned_users = sum(status == "cancelled"), churn_rate = churned_users / total_users ) The Failure: This code looks mathematically flawless. However, the business logic locally defined a “churned user” as someone who cancelled and didn’t reactivate within 14 days. The AI had no way of knowing this specific domain context. It relied on its training data to generate a generic, plausible definition of churn. The reporting was fundamentally wrong. This aligns with findings from software engineering studies showing that Large Language Models struggle with “implicit domain constraints”; rules that exist in the business logic but aren’t written explicitly in the database schema. 6.4 The Velocity Gap These logic failures contribute to what security researchers call the “Velocity Gap.” Because AI can pump out code incredibly fast, an analyst can generate 500 lines of data wrangling, feature engineering, and modeling scripts in an hour. This rapid production easily overwhelms existing peer review and QA processes. Critical vulnerabilities (such as exposed PII in logs or logically faulty data transformations) are created faster than human reviewers can detect them. To combat this, you cannot rely entirely on traditional review methods. In the next chapter, we look at how to formally audit AI-generated code.
auditing-ai-code-review-templates.html
Chapter 7 Auditing AI: Code Review Templates 7.1 The “Two-Gate” Mental Model 7.2 Demanding Evidence 7.3 The AI Code Review Template 7.4 Prompts for Code Review and Code Improvement

Chapter 7 Auditing AI: Code Review Templates Because AI models predict plausible code rather than deeply “understanding” business requirements, any output generated by GitHub Copilot must be treated as untrusted by default. Reviewing AI-generated code requires a fundamental shift in mindset. You must review the behavior, not just the syntax. AI code is usually well-formatted, which can mask dangerous logic. 7.1 The “Two-Gate” Mental Model When reviewing a script or a Pull Request heavily assisted by AI, separate your evaluation into two distinct gates: The Run Gate (Safety): Before you even assess if the code is logically correct, you must evaluate its impact; its “blast radius.” Does this code interact with a database? Does it write files to disk? Could it alter external states? Do not execute the code locally until you have cleared it through the Run Gate. The Ship Gate (Quality): Once deemed safe to run, review the logic, the edge cases, and whether it solves the actual business requirement. 7.2 Demanding Evidence A common mistake is reading AI code, seeing it has standard comments, and thinking “It looks like it handles null values.” Do not accept appearance as proof. Demand evidence. If Copilot writes a complex tidyr::pivot_wider() and aggregation pipeline, you or the reviewer must run unit tests or manual verification scripts showing what happens when the data contains NAs. 7.3 The AI Code Review Template To ensure these best practices are embedded in your team’s workflow, we recommend using a standardized review template. You can copy the following checklist into your Pull Request templates on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. 7.3.1 Pull Request Review: AI-Assisted Code 1. Safety Check (Run Gate) - [ ] No unsafe database writes or system modifications. - [ ] No untrusted dependencies introduced. - [ ] Sensitive PII data is not logged or exposed. 2. Logic & Data Integrity (Ship Gate) - [ ] Validates edge cases: handles missing values, zero-row tables, and duplicates. - [ ] Business logic strictly aligns with organizational definitions; no AI hallucinated metrics. - [ ] No silent data loss: filtering or joins don’t discard intended scope. 3. Quality & Evidence - [ ] Avoids reinventing internal utility functions. - [ ] Includes output logs or tests proving logic handles boundary cases correctly. Verdict: [ ] Approve | [ ] Request Changes | Confidence (1-5): ___ 7.3.2 Practical Language Examples When using the template above, you are looking for specific AI quirks based on the language. R Example: If an AI generates combined <- inner_join(sales, regions, by = "id"), the reviewer must enforce the Logic & Data Integrity check. inner_join drops unmatched records natively. The reviewer should request evidence that no critical regional data was silently dismissed, or demand the code be refactored to a left_join. Python Example: If an AI uses pandas and writes df['price'] = df['price'].fillna(method='ffill') for gap filling in time-series, the reviewer must catch the hallucinated metric check. Was forward-filling the correct domain treatment for pricing data, or will that skew financial aggregations artificially? The Reviewer should request alternative logic before approving. By enforcing this structure, you close the Velocity Gap. You ensure that the speed of vibe coding does not come at the expense of accuracy and reliability in your analytical output. 7.4 Prompts for Code Review and Code Improvement The following prompts are designed to be pasted directly into GitHub Copilot Chat, ChatGPT, or any LLM chat interface. Copy the relevant prompt, paste your code beneath it, and send. Think of these as structured checklists written in natural language — the AI will challenge its own output far more rigorously than it originally produced it. 7.4.1 Category 1: Safety and Impact Prompts Use these before running any unfamiliar AI-generated script. Blast Radius Check: “Review the following code and list every operation that could have a side effect outside this script. Flag any database writes, file system changes, API calls, or operations that modify state. Rate the overall risk as Low, Medium, or High.” Dependency Audit: “List every package or library imported or used in the following code. For each one, state what it does and whether it is a well-maintained, widely-used package or an obscure one that should be vetted.” PII and Privacy Scan: “Review this code for any place where sensitive personal information (names, emails, IDs, financial data) could be logged, printed, or written to a file. Flag each instance and suggest how to remove or anonymise it.” 7.4.2 Category 2: Logic and Data Integrity Prompts Use these to catch the silent failures that look correct on the surface. Join Type Interrogation: “This code contains one or more join operations. For each join, explain which rows would be dropped if the keys do not match. Tell me whether a left join, right join, inner join, or full join is most appropriate given the context, and why.” Null and Edge Case Stress Test: “Identify every place in this code where a missing value (NA in R, None/NaN in Python) could cause incorrect output or a silent failure. For each, suggest a defensive fix.” Aggregation Logic Audit: “Review all summarise(), groupby(), aggregate(), or equivalent operations. For each one, ask: Is this aggregation function (sum, mean, count) the right choice? Could duplicate rows distort the result? Could the grouping variable contain unexpected categories?” Data Scope Verification: “After each filter, join, or subset operation in this code, estimate how many rows should remain. Flag any step where row count could silently drop by more than 5% without an explicit validation check.” 7.4.3 Category 3: Domain Logic Prompts Use these to catch hallucinated business logic; the AI’s most common analytical failure. Business Rule Check: “This code calculates [metric name, e.g. monthly churn rate]. List every assumption this code makes about how that metric is defined. For each assumption, flag it as either ‘standard industry definition’ or ‘requires confirmation with the business’.” Time Period Validation: “Review all date filtering, windowing, or time-based aggregations. For each, confirm whether the date boundaries are inclusive or exclusive, and whether the fiscal calendar or the calendar year is being used.” Metric Reproducibility Check: “If I ran this code today and again in three months with the same input data, would I get identical results? List every potential source of non-determinism (random seeds, Sys.time(), current date dependencies, API calls).” 7.4.4 Category 4: Improvement and Refactoring Prompts Use these to actively improve code quality after a first-pass review. Code Simplification: “Review the following code and identify any unnecessarily complex logic that could be simplified. Suggest a refactored version that is easier to read and maintains identical behaviour.” Performance Review: “Identify any operations in this code that are computationally expensive and could be vectorised, parallelised, or replaced with a more efficient equivalent. Specifically look for row-wise loops that could be replaced with dplyr verbs in R or vectorised pandas operations in Python.” Test Generation: “Write unit tests for the following function. The tests should cover: (1) the normal happy path, (2) an input with all missing values, (3) an empty data frame or zero-row input, and (4) an input with duplicate keys.” Hostile Red-Team Review: “Act as a hostile senior data engineer reviewing this code before a production deployment. Find every possible way this code could produce incorrect output, crash, or expose data. Be thorough and adversarial.” 7.4.5 Category 5: Self-Explanation Prompts Use these when you receive AI-generated code you do not fully understand before running it. Line-by-Line Explanation: “Explain this code to me line by line in plain English. For each line, state what it does, why it is there, and what would happen if it were removed.” Assumption Surfacing: “List every assumption this code makes about the input data: its structure, column names, data types, and value ranges. For each assumption, tell me what error or wrong output would occur if that assumption were violated.”
managing-ai-teams-zero-trust-auditing.html

Chapter 8 Managing AI Teams: Zero-Trust Auditing 8.1 The Zero-Trust Review Mindset 8.2 How to Check the Work 8.3 How to Challenge the Work 8.4 A Note for Non-Technical Managers 8.5 Implementing Defensive Controls 8.6 Using AI to Assess AI 8.7 Closing the Loop

Chapter 8 Managing AI Teams: Zero-Trust Auditing If you are managing a team of data scientists or analysts using Vibe Analysis, you must adapt your leadership style to catch AI-accelerated failures. The industry consensus; driven by studies covering millions of lines of AI-assisted code; is moving definitively toward a Zero-Trust Review Mindset. Traditional code reviews assume human intent. A reviewer looks at the syntax and assumes the author understood the business requirements and edge cases. AI-generated code breaks this assumption entirely. Because LLMs predict plausible code rather than secure or domain-aware logic, managers must oversee the process as if reviewing work from a system that lacks any domain knowledge whatsoever. 8.1 The Zero-Trust Review Mindset “Zero-Trust” is a term borrowed from cybersecurity. It means: assume nothing is safe until proven otherwise. Applied to Vibe Analysis, it translates into three principles: Redefine “Done”: Explicitly state that “AI-generated” is never an excuse for tech debt or lower quality. All code must meet the same team standards (readability, security, maintainability) regardless of its origin. Explicit Human Ownership: Every piece of AI-generated code must have a clear human owner who can explain, debug, and maintain it. If an analyst cannot explain what a generated function does in plain language, it should not be merged. Review Behavior, Not Just Syntax: Traditional reviews focus on style and structure. For LLM code, focus on behavioral risks; how components interact, what happens under unusual conditions, and implicit assumptions about the data that the model may have overlooked. 8.2 How to Check the Work Managers shouldn’t be reading every single line of generated dplyr or pandas syntax. Instead, scale your review process by looking for specific evidence of human validation. Scan for Evidence: Reject PRs or analysis briefs that do not include “boundary-testing logs”; a technical term for proof that the code was tested at its extremes. For example: did they test what happens when a required category is completely missing from the data? Did they test what happens if a number is negative when it should only be positive? Demand actual test outputs (the “logs”) demonstrating this, rather than accepting tidy code comments as “proof” of safety. Check the Data Scope: AI frequently misinterprets filtering requirements. Always ask: “Did you verify that the row count matches exactly what we expect after this join?” Detect “Refactoring Fatigue”: Watch for PRs where AI has rewritten large sections of working code for no clear reason. The GitClear 2024 study (“Coding on Copilot”) found that as AI assistance increases, “code churn”; the percentage of code reverted or updated within two weeks; grows rapidly. Reject unnecessary rewrites; they increase the risk of subtle bugs. 8.3 How to Challenge the Work Because AI-generated code looks neat and professional, managers must actively challenge the underlying domain logic rather than taking well-formatted output at face value. Ask Adversarial Questions: During reviews, probe the logic: “Why did we choose an inner_join here instead of a left_join?” or “What happens to this calculation if the pricing data is missing for an entire region?” An analyst practicing true Vibe Analysis can answer immediately. An analyst caught in the “Illusion of Productivity” will stumble. Assumption Checking: Does the generated code make implicit assumptions about inputs, data size, or the environment? Require these assumptions to be written down explicitly in the PR description or analysis brief. 8.4 A Note for Non-Technical Managers If you manage a data team but do not write R or Python code yourself, you are not at a disadvantage; you are actually uniquely positioned to be the ultimate backstop against AI hallucinations. Your job is not to review the syntax, but to review the assumptions and the conclusions. 8.4.1 Ask the “Stupid” Domain Questions AI models lack the nuanced, unwritten rules of your specific business. These rules live in your head, not in any database schema. You are the only person who can catch these failures: “Does this code know that our fiscal year starts in July, not January?” “How does this pipeline handle the legacy VIP accounts from before 2021?” “Are we counting trial users in the total, or only paid subscribers?” If the analyst hesitates or says “I think so,” that is a red flag. They need to verify with evidence, not assumptions. 8.4.2 Focus on the Data Journey Instead of asking how they coded it, ask them to trace the journey of a single piece of data through the pipeline. For example: “Walk me through exactly what happens to a missing transaction value from the moment it enters the script to the final aggregation.” If they cannot trace it in plain English, they are relying too heavily on Copilot’s generation and have not truly reviewed the logic. 8.4.3 Request a One-Page Summary For major deliverables, require that the analyst provides a brief, non-technical summary alongside their code. This summary should state: What data was used and from where. What was excluded and why. What assumptions were made. What the key numbers are and whether they pass a basic “smell test” (do they align with what you would roughly expect?). This forces the analyst to step outside the code and think critically about what it actually produced. 8.5 Implementing Defensive Controls Human review is essential, but it doesn’t scale perfectly when AI can generate hundreds of lines per hour. To stay ahead, build automated safety nets into your team’s workflow. Automated Quality Gates: Use code checking tools (called “linters”) that automatically scan for common errors, style violations, and security issues. In R, a popular one is lintr; in Python, it is ruff. These tools flag problems before a human reviewer even looks at the PR, saving significant time. Protecting Sensitive Data: Ensure your analysts use .copilotignore files to explicitly exclude sensitive datasets, API keys, or PII from being indexed by Copilot. If your organization handles regulated data, ensure you are using Enterprise tiers of AI tools to guarantee your proprietary data is not used to train public models. Audit for “Shadow AI”: Maintain visibility into which AI tools are being used across the team. Unsanctioned tools; a free ChatGPT account used to process client data, for example; can introduce serious compliance and data privacy risks. 8.6 Using AI to Assess AI Ironically, one of the most effective ways to audit AI-generated code is to use another AI to challenge it. You can leverage LLMs to stress-test the submitted work. Take the analyst’s final code and feed it into Copilot Chat or ChatGPT with prompts such as: “Act as a hostile code reviewer. Find any implicit assumptions, missing data handlers, or logical flaws in this data pipeline.” Or, for a more targeted check: “This code calculates monthly churn. List every edge case where it could produce an incorrect number.” While AI struggles to write perfect business logic from scratch, it is surprisingly adept at identifying missing edge cases, potential null handling issues, and logical dead-ends in existing code. Use the AI to find the cracks, but rely on your human analysts to seal them. A word of caution: Do not treat the AI reviewer’s output as gospel either. It will sometimes flag non-issues or miss context-specific problems. The goal is to generate a starting list of questions to bring back to the analyst, not to fully automate the review. 8.7 Closing the Loop The core message of this chapter is simple: the speed of Vibe Analysis demands a proportional increase in oversight discipline. As a manager, your role is shifting from “Did they write clean code?” to “Did they understand what the AI wrote for them?” If you embed these zero-trust principles into your team’s workflow; adversarial questioning, boundary-testing evidence, non-technical summaries, and automated safety nets; you will capture the immense productivity benefits of AI without exposing your organisation to silent analytical failures.
conclusion.html
Chapter 9 Conclusion 9.1 Becoming an Architect

Chapter 9 Conclusion Vibe Analysis is fundamentally changing what it means to be a data analyst or data scientist. By abstracting away the syntax, GitHub Copilot allows us to operate closer to the speed of thought. However, that speed is dangerous without discipline. 9.1 Becoming an Architect As you adopt this workflow, remember that the AI is your fast-typing assistant. Keep the following principles in mind: Never surrender the logic. You are responsible for the statistical validity of the analysis. If you don’t know what a block of code does, don’t run it. Use Copilot Chat to say, “Explain this code to me line by line.” Context is everything. The better you describe your data and your intent, the better the AI’s output will be. Validate continuously. Check your row counts after joins. Check your distributions after mutations. Embrace the conversational workflow. When you hit an error, don’t revert to manual debugging immediately. Paste the error into the chat and let the assistant figure it out. Consider a scenario of a junior analyst tasked with forecasting Q4 sales. A year ago, they would have spent two hours fighting ggplot2 themes to get a legend aligned horizontally, and another three hours debugging a lubridate time zone issue. Today, using Vibe Analysis, they generate the chart and date logic in five minutes. Because they saved five hours of manual typing, they have the cognitive energy to actually look at the output. They notice a strange dip in November. Digging deeper, they realize the AI-generated data cleaning script silently omitted a specific region due to an implicit inner_join. They didn’t just ship the code; they reviewed the logic, found the bias, and fixed it before the executive presentation. That is the role of an Architect. You spend less time wrestling with commas and more time wrestling with truth. The goal of learning R or Python is no longer to be a walking syntax dictionary. The goal is to understand how to manipulate data conceptually so that you can effectively guide your AI to write the syntax for you. Welcome to the future of data analysis. Happy Vibe Analysis.
